speaker: interviewer
text: Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Brussels again in 1939. It shows Heinz, my brother, Heinz Geiringer, with me. It was his first-- in Brussels, velo, which he got. And you can see he's very-- we are both very proud of it. This was the summer of 1939. That's my father, Erich Geiringer, and my mother, Fritzi Geiringer, on a holiday on the Belgian coast. This is Amsterdam Merwedeplein where we moved in February 1940 in our flight. We lived on the left-hand side of this photo on the first floor. And the Franks lived on the right-hand side on the second floor. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What is interesting to see that how few cars are there. At the time, nobody had cars. I went-- when I went back there, there were about 100 cars. The whole road was full. For every house on both sides of the road was all cars, cars, cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back there, was that after--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, yeah, recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, recently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not post-war.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no. I think two or three people had cars then.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is as well on the Merwedeplein. That was my first bicycle I got. And in the back, is on a scooter, is my friend [? Janny. ?] She was a very clever girl, and we were very good friends. All her family, her brother, mother, and sister, and parents were all killed. This is Heinz Geiringer, my brother, sitting on the-- on the stoop of our flat in-- on the Merwedeplein playing his guitar. He was a wonderful musician. You could give him any instrument, and he could play it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That could have been taken in 1941, I think. Father Erich Geiringer, it was a enlarged passport photograph. I think it was his last photo which has been taken, so it must have been in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam. This is my-- the-- my father's, Erich Geiringer's, passport, which was the last one he got. You can see on the top corner, you can see a J, which all Jews had to be put in. And you can see after his name, Erich, they put Israel. All males had to be called Israel, and all girls or women had to be called Sara on their passports. And Sylvia wanted to call her daughter Sara, and I objected to that because it would remind me too much of my name I got unvoluntarily. On the right, you can see that we children were put into my father's passport. Again, the addition of Israel for my brother and Sara for me has been added. A painting which Heinz Geiringer made when he was in hiding. He imagines him to be outside again and sailing his little boat. He had a little boat. And that was when he dyed his hair blond, and it came out rather light, and he was quite upset about that. And he painted himself then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- oh, we had a lot of paintings. And when they escaped in that hiding place from this woman in the night, they hid all their things under the floorboards. And after the war, my mother and me went back to this house, and there were also people living there. And we told them the story. And we said, could we look under the floor? And they let us, of course. And we found all the paintings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that how you got the photographs, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the photographs were all photographs which we had sent to my grandparents. This is me, Eva Geiringer, in my first school in Amsterdam. That was a school photo, which been taken. You can see in the background is a photo of the Dutch colony Indonesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941. This is our registration made in Auschwitz in 1945 by the Russians. So they give us a certificate that we could show later that we had been there in Auschwitz. This is our, Zvi and my, wedding photo, which was taken in Amsterdam in 1951. On the left-hand side is my mother, Fritzi Geiringer; then Otto Frank; then me; then my husband, Zvi Schloss; then Zvi's mother, [? Ilda ?] Schloss; then my grandmother, Helene Markovits. And that is-- was our cleaning lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know her name anymore. This is my mother, Fritzi Frank, and Otto Frank's wedding photo. It was taken in Amsterdam in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo taken in Switzerland in-- where my mother and Otto lived in Basel. On the left-hand side is my mother, Fritzi Frank. In her arms, she holds Sylvia Schloss, our youngest daughter. Underneath, you see Jacky Schloss, and next to her, Caroline Schloss. Then in the middle is my grandmother, Helene Markovits, and then me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1962. This is a photo of me, Eva Geiringer, taken in 1948 in Amsterdam. This is a photo of Mini Sameck, who saved our lives. This is taken in Israel in about 1965. She emigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make contact with her after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she knew-- she got in contact with us. She went back to Prague. And then through the Red Cross, I think, they heard that we were back in Amsterdam. This is a memorial which has been erected in Amsterdam from the Liberal Jewish Community. It's a huge-- it's very, very big war memorial with all the names of the people who belonged to the community on it. You can see there my father's name, Erich Geiringer, and underneath, Heinz Geiringer. Under F you will find Edith Frank, Margot Frank, and Anne Frank. Yeah, now you are there. This is a photograph taken of my family in Kenwood, Hampstead Heath, for my 65th birthday, so in 1960-- '64. This is Eric Hovelsen, Jacky's son, and Lisa Hovelsen. And that is Jacky Hovelsen. Behind it, you can see Caroline Margolis and Alexander Margolis. And that is Sylvia Schloss with Sophie Schloss. The new baby's not on yet. This is a photograph taken in 1995, outside St. Paul's Cathedral in London. It was a memorial service in honor or Anne Frank in St. Paul's Cathedral. And it is my mother, Fritzi Frank, and myself. This is Sophie, Sophie Yaron and Ella Yaron. That's the children of Sylvia Schloss and Gil Yaron, our youngest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ella.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ella. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did say Ella, didn't I? I did say Ella. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Harpenden, where they live. And it was taken in 1969.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my story, which I wrote in 1988 and has been published in England, Holland, Germany, Norway, Sweden, and Japan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It tells all about my story about before the war, the war, and after the war. And this is a photograph of my mother, Fritzi Frank, in our house in Dorset Drive. This is a porcelain bear, which my father bought in, perhaps, in 1935, '36 in Vienna at an auction. He was very keen on going to auctions. And he always bid much more than he intended to bid for. And it's one of few things from our goods which survived, because we all were very attached to it. And my grandmother took it to England. And after the war, she gave it back to my mother. And now recently, my mother asked me what I wanted from her things, and I chose the bear. And so he's come to live with us now. Here is, as well, it reminds me of when we were liberated from Russia in Auschwitz by the Russians. The first thing I saw was a Russian soldier covered in fur and snow. And I thought at the time it was a bear at the gates. So he's very dear to me.
